content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 7 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2CO_007_page_001.jpg

1. Therefore having these promises, beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from every defilement of flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in [the] fear of God.

2. Make room for us. We wronged no one, we corrupted no one, we took advantage of no one.

3. I do not say [this] for condemnation, for I have said before that you are in the hearts of us, to die together and to live together.

4. Great [is] my boldness toward you, great [is] my boasting on behalf of you. I have been filled with the comfort, I am overflowing with the joy in all the affliction of us.

5. For indeed, [when] we came into Makedonia, the flesh of us had no rest, but [we were] afflicted in every [way] — battles outside, fears within.

6. But the [one] comforting the lowly, God, comforted us by the coming of Titos,

7. and not only by his coming but also by the comfort with which he was comforted over you, reporting to us the longing of you, the mourning of you, the zeal of you for me, so that I rejoiced even more.

8. Because even if I grieved you by the letter, I do not regret [it]. Even if I did regret [it] — for I see that that letter grieved you, even [if only] for an hour —

9. now I rejoice, not that you were grieved, but that you were grieved to repentance. For you were grieved according to God, that in nothing you might suffer loss from us.

10. For grief according to God produces repentance to salvation, not to be regretted; but the grief of the world produces death.

11. For behold this very [thing], your being grieved according to God, what earnestness it produced in you, what defense, what indignation, what fear, what longing, what zeal, what punishment! In everything you demonstrated yourselves to be pure in the matter.

12. So then, even [though] I wrote to you, [it was] not for the sake of the [one] who wronged, nor for the sake of the [one] who was wronged, but for the sake of the earnestness of you on behalf of us being made manifest to you before God.

13. Therefore we have been comforted. And in addition to our comfort, we rejoiced even more abundantly at the joy of Titos, because his spirit has been refreshed by all of you.

14. For if I have boasted anything to him about you, I was not put to shame. But just as we spoke all [things] to you in truth, so also the boasting of us before Titos proved to be truth.

15. And the affections of him abound even more to you, [as he] remembers the obedience of all of you, as with fear and trembling you received him.

16. I rejoice that in everything I am confident in you.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "defilement of flesh and spirit":** μολυσμοῦ σαρκὸς καὶ πνεύματος — defilement affects the whole person (both physical and spiritual dimensions). This completes the thought begun in 6:14-18.

2. **v.5 — "battles outside, fears within":** ἔξωθεν μάχαι, ἔσωθεν φόβοι — a compressed expression of Paul's situation in Macedonia. External conflicts (probably with opponents) and internal anxiety (about the Corinthians).

3. **v.10 — "grief according to God" vs. "grief of the world":** λύπη κατὰ θεόν / λύπη τοῦ κόσμου — Paul distinguishes two kinds of grief. Godly grief leads to repentance and salvation; worldly grief leads to death. The distinction is not emotional but directional.

4. **v.11 — Seven results of godly grief:** σπουδή (earnestness), ἀπολογία (defense), ἀγανάκτησις (indignation), φόβος (fear), ἐπιπόθησις (longing), ζῆλος (zeal), ἐκδίκησις (punishment). This sevenfold list demonstrates the completeness of the Corinthians' response.

5. **v.12 — The purpose of the tearful letter:** Paul reveals his deeper motive — not punishment of the offender but the Corinthians' own self-revelation. The letter was a test of their loyalty and earnestness.

— EMET, AI Translator
